lines.text: This is a demo of the Smart Lab Digital Twin, a real-time digital twin of a laboratory room. A physics simulator models the room's temperature, humidity, and occupancy, then streams that telemetry over MQTT to a Streamlit dashboard and an interactive Three.js 3D view.

Crucially, this is a true digital twin, not just a digital shadow. Commands flow back from the dashboard to the simulator, closing the loop. And a PID controller automatically regulates the air conditioning power, to hold any target temperature.

This is the main dashboard. At the top, live metrics: room temperature, humidity, and occupancy, updated every few seconds over MQTT. Here is the HVAC status panel, showing whether the AC is on and its cooling power as a percentage. Below, a temperature trend chart with a predictive alert, and an inline 3D view of the room. On the left, the control panel: AC on and off, target setpoint, occupancy override, and simulation time-scale.

Let's simulate a busy lab session. Occupancy is overridden to twenty-seven people. Each person adds heat, roughly a hundred watts, so the room now carries nearly three kilowatts of internal load. With the AC off, the temperature climbs steadily, and the predictive alert warns that the room will overheat if nothing changes.

Now the AC turns on, with a twenty-two degree setpoint. The PID controller takes over. It measures the error between room temperature and setpoint, and modulates AC power continuously, from zero to one hundred percent of its three and a half kilowatt capacity. With a large error, the proportional term drives the AC to full power.

As the temperature approaches the setpoint, power backs off smoothly, instead of crudely switching on and off. And here is the key result: the temperature locks onto exactly twenty-two degrees, even under a steady occupant load. The integral term eliminates steady-state offset, and the derivative term damps overshoot.

Watch what happens when the setpoint changes. The dashboard doesn't just assume the new value. It publishes a command over MQTT, the simulator applies it, and the confirmed state comes back and updates the display. That round trip is what makes this a true digital twin, rather than a one-way shadow.

The 3D view is built with Three.js, and subscribes to the same MQTT topics directly from the browser over WebSockets. People walk in and out through the automatic glass doors as occupancy changes, and the floor color encodes temperature: blue at setpoint, shifting toward red as the room heats up.

Everything is driven by open MQTT topics, so any client can join. Here, occupancy is raised straight from the command line, and the dashboard jumps to twenty-nine occupants almost instantly. If the simulator ever disconnects, the broker's Last Will and Testament immediately marks the twin offline.

To recap: a physics-based room simulator, event-driven MQTT with retained state and failure detection, a live dashboard and 3D visualization, and a closed feedback loop with an auto-regulating PID controller. Together, they form a complete, real-time digital twin of a smart laboratory. Thanks for watching.
